coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence detection of malachite green in fish tissue using red emissive Se,N,Cl-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yaoping
Gao, Zhijin
Luo, Junfei
coredata.dc:description: Facile detection of malachite green (MG), a toxic dye, in aquaculture is urgently demanded for environment and food safety. Herein, we design a novel fluorescent probe, namely red emissive Se,N,Cl-doped carbon dots (CDs), to accurately determinate MG. CDs are prepared by hydrothermal treatment of selenourea and o-phenylenediamine in HCl solution. This material exhibits excitation-independent dual emissions at 625 and 679 nm, with a high quantum yield of 23.6%. A selective and sensitive fluorescent sensor toward MG is established based on inner filter effect, because both the excitation and emission light of CDs can be strongly absorbed by MG. The fluorescence quenching of CDs is linear to the MG concentration over the range of 0.07–2.50 μM with a low detection limit of 21 nM. Trace-level analysis of MG in fish tissue is successfully explored, demonstrating the great potential of the proposed sensor for MG monitoring in aquatic products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Malachite green
Red emissive carbon dots
Fluorescent sensor
Inner filter effect
Fish tissue

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of MG in fish tissue samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A Schematic of preparation of red emissive Se,N,Cl-doped CDs and their application for MG detection based on fluorescence quenching. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) Wide-area TEM image, (b) high-resolution TEM image, (c) XRD pattern, and (d) FTIR spectrum of the red emissive CDs. Inset in (a): size distribution of CDs.

XPS spectra for (a) survey scan, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) Se3d of the red emissive CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) PL spectra of the red emissive CDs. Insets in (a): photographs of the aqueous CD dispersion in daylight and under 560 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) PL spectra of the red emissive CDs in the presence of MG at different concentrations (0.00, 0.04, 0.10, 0.20, 0.40, 0.60, 1.00, 1.50, 2.00, 2.50, 3.00, 4.00, 5.00, 7.00, and 10.00 μM). Inset shows the linear relationship between the quenching effect (F0/F) and MG concentration in the range of 0–2.50 μM. The tests were repeated for six times. F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CDs at 625 nm in the absence and presence of MG, respectively. (b) Absorption spectrum of MG, PL and PLE spectra of the red emissive CDs. (c) PL decay profiles of the red emissive CDs in the presence and absence of MG. The MG concentration is 2.5 μM, and the experiments are done at room temperature (20 °C) (d) Selectivity of the CD-based fluorescent sensor for MG. The concentrations of all the analytes are 2.5 μM. The concentration of the red emissive CDs is 10 mg L−1. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The analytical results for MG detection in fish tissue samples using the CD-based fluorescence method and HPLC.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence detection of malachite green in fish tissue using red emissive Se,N,Cl-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Chemical Engineering, Ningbo University, Ningbo 315211, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ningbo Institute of Materials Technology and Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Ningbo 315201, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Facile detection of malachite green (MG), a toxic dye, in aquaculture is urgently demanded for environment and food safety. Herein, we design a novel fluorescent probe, namely red emissive Se,N,Cl-doped carbon dots (CDs), to accurately determinate MG. CDs are prepared by hydrothermal treatment of selenourea and o-phenylenediamine in HCl solution. This material exhibits excitation-independent dual emissions at 625 and 679 nm, with a high quantum yield of 23.6%. A selective and sensitive fluorescent sensor toward MG is established based on inner filter effect, because both the excitation and emission light of CDs can be strongly absorbed by MG. The fluorescence quenching of CDs is linear to the MG concentration over the range of 0.07–2.50 μM with a low detection limit of 21 nM. Trace-level analysis of MG in fish tissue is successfully explored, demonstrating the great potential of the proposed sensor for MG monitoring in aquatic products.

ce:section.ce:para: Malachite green (MG) is a triarylmethane dye that has been extensively used for coloring purpose in leather, silk, cotton and paper industries (Khan et al., 2019). It can also serve as an effective fungicide and parasiticide in aquaculture (Samiey & Toosi, 2010). However, MG is highly toxic. It may cause carcinogenicity and teratogenicity on animal and human body, and thus is explicitly prohibited in fish farming (Yu et al., 2017). But, the illegal use of MG frequently happens in some countries due to its high efficacy and low cost (Zhang et al., 2015). Therefore, the facile detection of MG is in a strong demand for environment and food safety.

Over the past several years, various approaches have been set up to determinate MG, including surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) (Deng et al., 2019; Lai et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2019), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Chen & Miao, 2010), immunoassay (Wang et al., 2016), colorimetry (Gavrilenko et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2019), electrochemical analysis (Wu et al., 2020), molecular imprinting technique (Lin et al., 2016), and fluorescence method (Ju et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2019). Among them, fluorescence method has the great advantages of rapidness, sensitiveness, and easy operation. Very recently, Li group realized a ratiometric fluorescence test of MG based on dual-emissive gold nanoclusters through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and dynamic quenching (Ju et al., 2018). Luo et al exploited an RNA aptamer to specially bind with MG for its fluorescence “off–on” detection (Luo et al., 2019). Except for a few impressive cases, the fluorescence assay of MG is rarely explored, due to the limited development of excellent fluorescent sensors toward MG.

Carbon dots (CDs), an emerging photoluminescent nanomaterial, have attracted increasing attention in both fundamental and technological fields because of their fascinating chemical, physical, and optical properties (Dhenadhayalan et al., 2020; Hu et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2020). The photoluminescence (PL) of CDs, however, is commonly found in blue and green region, while the yellow and red emissive CDs are rather difficult to obtain (Shi et al., 2019; Su et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2019). Due to the importance of the red light in biological and sensing applications, it is essential to extend the emission of CDs to long-wavelength region, i.e., greater than 600 nm.

In the present paper, we report an acidic hydrothermal synthesis of red emissive Se,N,Cl-doped CDs using selenourea, o-phenylenediamine, and hydrochloric acid as reagents (Fig. 1 ). The as-prepared CDs exhibits two emission peaks at 625 and 679 nm, with an improved quantum yield of up to 23.6%, which is much higher than that of recently used gold nanoclusters (1.4%) (Ju et al., 2018). Based on an efficient inner filter effect (IFE), the red fluorescence of CDs can be remarkably and selectively quenched by MG. Thus, a fluorescent sensor toward MG is developed. Such a sensor is also applied to detect MG in fish tissue sample, and satisfactory results are achieved.

Selenourea was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. o-Phenylenediamine, malachite green (MG), glucose, dopamine, ascorbic acid, uric acid, cysteine, curcumin, lemon yellow, Sudan I, congo red, and crystal violet were purchased from Aladdin. Other chemicals were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

Briefly, 50 mg of o-phenylenediamine and 10 mg of selenourea were added in 30 mL of 50 mM HCl solution. After sonication for 10 min, the mixture was transferred to a 50-mL autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 6 h, to yield a dark blue dispersion of CDs. For purification of the product, the resulting dispersion was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, passed through a 0.22-μm membrane filter, and dialyzed in a 1000-Da dialysis bag. The solid CDs were gained by freeze-drying.

An aliquot of red emissive CDs (1 mg mL−1, 20 μL) was added to phosphate buffer (100 mM, pH 7.4, 1980 μL) containing different concentrations of MG. After vigorously shaking for 1 min to enable thorough mixing, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded (excitation 560 nm, maximum emission 625 nm). The interferences of other chemicals were investigated individually in the presence of CDs. The tests were repeated for six times.

Three kinds of edible fishes, namely carp, weever and grouper, were bought from the local market near Ningbo University. The fish tissue samples were pretreated as described elsewhere with some modification (Luo et al., 2019). In a typical run, 10 g of the back muscle was minced and put into a 500-mL beaker. Then, 30 mL of acetonitrile and 120 mL of the phosphate buffer containing different concentrations of MG were added into the beaker. After 1-hour sonification and 10-minute centrifugation at 8000 rpm, the supernatant was collected. Finally, 20 μL of the CD dispersion (1 mg mL−1) was mixed with the 1980 μL of the as-prepared sample for fluorescence measurement.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM images of CDs were observed on the JEM-2100 apparatus (JEOL, Japan) with a CCD camera (resolution: 4008 × 2672 maximum) operating at 200 kV. The Raman spectrum was measured on the inVia-reflex spectroscopy (Renishaw, UK) using a 325 nm laser. The Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectrum was acquired on the Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were made on the AXIS UL TRA DLD system (Shimadzu, Japan) using Al/Kα radiation. The Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were monitored in 1-cm quartz cuvettes at room temperature (20 °C) using the double beam UV-3101PC spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan), and the deionized water was used as the blank. The fluorescence spectra were measured in a 1-cm quartz cuvette at room temperature (20 °C) using the F-7000 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Both the excitation and emission slits were 10 nm, and the applied voltage to the photomultiplier was set at 600 V. All the fluorescence spectra were corrected. The time-resolved fluorescence spectra were taken on the FL3-111 spectrometer (Horiba, France) at room temperature (20 °C). The maximum counts at the peak of the transient were set at 500. The full width of half-maximum (FWHM) of the instrument response function was about 2 ns. The excitation and emission wavelengths were set at 560 and 625 nm, respectively. The lifetimes were obtained from the Horiba software. The HPLC measurement was performed on the 1260 HPLC equipment (Agilent, USA), and the detailed description could be found in the Supplementary Material.

As shown in the wide-area TEM image (Fig. 2 a), the small black dots represent the as-synthesized nanomaterials. They are nearly monodisperse without apparent aggregation. Their particle sizes range from 1 to 5 nm with the maximum population at 3 nm (Fig. 2a, inset). The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2b) reveals the amorphous structure of these dots, because no clear lattice fringe is observed. In the Raman spectrum (Fig. 2c), the D and G bands at 1332 and 1573 cm−1 correspond to the sp3 defects and sp2 carbon, respectively. The high intensity ratio of ID/IG (0.77) indicating the existence of many structural defects within sp2 clusters (Kundu et al., 2020). Fig. 2d gives the FTIR spectrum of the sample. The characteristic bands at 3132, 2827 and 1683 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH/NH, CH and CO, respectively. Several peaks at 1610, 1579, 1528, and 1501 can be assigned to the stretching vibrations of aromatic CC/CN bonds. Additionally, the CN bond at 1403 cm−1, CO bond in the region of 1037–1241 cm−1, out-plane deformation of aromatic CH at 752 cm−1, and CCl bond at 586 cm−1 are visualized. The FTIR interpretations are confirmed by the XPS results. The survey spectrum (Fig. 3 a) shows the obvious peaks of O1s, N1s, C1s, Cl2s and Cl2p at 530, 399, 284, 268 and 198 eV, respectively. A tiny amount of Se3d at 57 eV is also detectable. The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 3b) can be fitted into five carbon species: CC/CC at 284.5 eV, CN at 285.6 eV, CO at 286.3 eV, CCl at 287.3 eV, and CO/CN at 287.9 eV (Li et al., 2013; Liu et al., 2018). In the N1s spectrum (Fig. 3c), four nitrogen components, corresponding to pyridinic N at 398.5 eV, pyrrolic N at 399.5 eV, graphitic N at 400.5 eV, and NH at 401.6 eV, are presented (Tetsuka et al., 2016). The Se3d spectrum (Fig. 3d) is resolved into two weak signals at 56.1 and 57.0 eV, proving the existence of CSe and CSeSe bonds (Xu et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2015). To summarize all the characterization data, the as-synthesized samples are Se,N,Cl-doped carbogenic nanodots, which have aromatic constituents in the core and functional groups including hydroxyl, carboxyl, amino, and chlorine on the surface.

The aqueous dispersion of CDs is blue in daylight (Fig. 4 a, inset), quite different from the yellow color of typical CD solutions. It emits a brilliant red fluorescence under the excitation of 560 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 4a) exhibits a narrow peak at 227 nm and a broad band at around 279 nm in the UV range, which are attributed to the π–π* transition of aromatic units and n–π* transitions of carbonyl/hydroxyl moieties, respectively (Chen, Li et al., 2018). In the visible region of Fig. 4a, two sharp peaks at 561 and 612 nm are related to the low-energy surface state (Pan et al., 2016; Sharma et al., 2017). These CDs have a predominant emission peak at 625 nm and a weaker one at 679 nm (Fig. 4b). The peak positions are independent of excitation wavelength. The PL excitation (PLE) spectrum shows two features at 564 and 614 nm, very similar to those observed in the UV–vis absorption curve (Fig. S1), revealing that the low-energy surface state, rather than core structure, is responsible for the red emission (Chen et al., 2017). The PL decay prolife (Fig. S2) is appropriately fitted by a biexponential formula, giving an average lifetime of 7.69 ns. The relative quantum yield of CDs is calculated to be 23.6% by comparison with rhodamine 6G (The detailed calculation method is described in the Supplementary Material). Such a value is outstanding among the reported red emissive CDs (Arcudi et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2017; Ge et al., 2015; Ostadhossein et al., 2020; Pan et al., 2016; Su et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2018). The fluorescence intensity of CDs remains nearly unchanged under continuous light excitation for 4 h (Fig. S3) and at high ionic strength (0.5 M, NaCl) (Fig. S4), indicating the good photostability, which benefits their sensing applications.

With superior optical properties, the red emissive CDs are utilized to detect MG through fluorescence quenching. Under the optimal conditions (Figs. S5–S7), the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 625 nm decreases by 33.8% when 1 μM of MG is introduced. Fig. 5 a depicts a dose–response graph to determine the relationship between the quenching effect (F0/F) and the concentration of MG. Upon increasing the concentration of MG from 0 to 10 μM, the fluorescence intensity of CDs gradual declines. In the range of 0–2.50 μM, a fitted linear equation can be expressed as F0/F = 0.515 × C MG (μM) + 1.002 (R2 = 0.9997). The limit of detection (LOD) is measured to be 21 nM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, lower than those obtained using other fluorescent probes (Ju et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2017). The limit of quantitation (LOQ) is 70 nM, and thus the linear detection range is 0.07–2.50 μM. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching is carefully investigated. As shown in Fig. 5b, the absorption band of MG has a quite precise overlapping with the excitation and emission spectra of CDs, indicating that MG can effectively absorb both the excitation and emission light of CDs, so the fluorescence quenching may be resulted from FRET or IFE (Sun & Lei, 2017; Chen, Yu, & Wang, 2018). Generally, for FRET, the quencher shortens the fluorescence lifetime of the donor, while for IFE, it does not (Zhang et al., 2019). Fig. 5c illustrates the unchanged fluorescence decay profile of CDs after adding MG, excluding the possibility of FRET, and demonstrating the main quenching mechanism from IFE. The fluorescence responses of CDs toward potential interferents are evaluated (Fig. 5d). Various metal ions, including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Al3+, cause negligible fluorescence alterations. Several physiological compounds, such as glucose, dopamine, ascorbic acid, uric acid, and cysteine, also have no interference. Among organic dyes like curcumin, lemon yellow, Sudan I, congo red, and crystal violet, only crystal violet slightly suppresses the fluorescence. These results confirm the high selectivity of CDs toward MG. Compared with other methods for MG determination (Table S1) (Deng et al., 2019; Chen & Miao, 2010; Gavrilenko et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020; Lin et al., 2016; Ju et al., 2018; Luo et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2017), the present one is sensitive, selective, fast, and simple.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of MG in fish tissue samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practical applicability of our analytical strategy was assessed by estimating the spiked concentrations of MG in three fish tissue samples using standard addition method. HPLC was employed for comparison (Li et al., 2015). As shown in Table 1 , using the CDs as a fluorescent sensor, the recoveries ranges from 92.0% to 108.0% with relative standard deviations (RSDs) below 5.9%. Such results are quite close to those obtained from HPLC, proving the high feasibility of the established method for MG detection in real samples.

In summary, we have developed a hydrothermal method for the synthesis of red emissive Se,N,Cl-doped CDs with a respectable quantum yield of 23.6%. The emission peak of CDs at 625 nm can be selectively quenched by MG with minor or negligible interference from many other substances, based on the mechanism of IFE. The linearity for MG detection is in the range of 0.07–2.50 μM, and the LOD is measured to be as low as 21 nM. Such a fluorescent sensor is able to quantitatively determine the trace amount of MG in fish tissue samples. This work offers a simple synthetic route to bright CDs with red fluorescence, and illustrates their potential utility for monitoring toxicant in aqueous environment and food.

Yaoping Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Zhijin Gao: Investigation, Validation, Data curation. Junfei Luo: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province (LQ20E020005), the State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science (SKLACLS2004), the Natural Science Foundation of Ningbo City (2019A610188, 2019A610027), and the K. C. Wong Magna Fund in Ningbo University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.127677.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
